1: <header>Life cycle assessment of anaerobic digestion of pig manure coupled with different digestate treatment technologies </header>
Na Duan , Benyamin Khoshnevisan , Cong Lin Zhidan Liu Hongbin Liu

a Laboratory of Environment-Enhancing Energy (E2E) and Key Laboratory of Agricultural Engineering in Structure and Environment, Ministry of Agriculture, College of Water Resources and Civil Engineering, China Agricultural University, Beijing 100083, China b Key Laboratory of Non-point Source Pollution Control, Ministry of Agriculture, Institute of Agricultural Resources and Regional Planning, Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Beijing 100081, China

ARTICLE INFO|ABSTRACT Handling Editor: Dr. Zhen Jason He Keywords: Anaerobic digestion Digestate treatment Flocculation-biological contact oxidation Microalgae|The direct use of digestate on farmlands as soil amendment is becoming an uneconomic option for farmers. Moreover, there are serious environmental concerns about its oversupply in regions with intensive biogas plants. Downstream technologies, offering innovative upcycling methods to handle huge amounts of digestate, have absorbed great interest in this context. In this study, three digestate treatment technologies were compared from a life cycle assessment perspective to combine the environmental impacts from pig manure transportation to biogas plants, biogas production, different digestate treatment technologies, and the use of final products. The results showed that scenario including digestate fractionation into solid and liquid, and their use for compost production and microalgae cultivation, respectively, would be a suitable downstream strategy with lower im- pacts on human health, ecosystem quality, and climate change damage categories, however future improve- ments still required. The results showed that sealed storage system or fast-continuous downstream processes as well as shorter distances between biogas plants and farms can significantly enhance the environmental per- formance of coupled anaerobic digestion and microalgae production. The high energy payback also signified that co-digestion of pig manure and microalgae would be energetically favorable in this context. However, having compared the results with a baseline scenario demonstrated that the direct use of digestate on farmlands, under controlled conditions to avoid its over application, is still the most environmentally favorable option, despite being a costly option for farmers. The results achieved in the present study suffered some uncertainties because technologies under consideration are at their infancy stage, thus further research still is required to find the most sustainable solutions.
2: China is the largest pig producer in the world with an average pig rearing amount of 449 million heads per year (FAOSTST, 2019). The number of large-scale and intensive pig farms has drastically increased in China while most of them are not supplemented by adequate manure management strategies to handle the large amounts of manure pro- duced (Duan et al., 2019; Jiang et al., 2011). To address the concerns over pig manure (PM) mismanagement and prevent associated en- vironmental pollutions such as CH4 and NH3 emissions as well as the related damages like eutrophication and soil acidification, a series of incentive policies and regulations have been implemented (Zhang et al., 2013). In this context, anaerobic digestion (AD) of manure has been

introduced as the most environmentally-friendly solution which aims at mitigating the above-mentioned problems while simultaneously pro- ducing bioenergy (biogas) and high-quality bio-fertilizers (Khoshnevisan and Angelidaki, 2018; Li et al., 2017).

The development of medium and large-scale biogas plants has ac- celerated thanks to the implemented incentive policies, the increased environmental awareness of farm owners, and the improved profit- ability of AD plants (Hu et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2012). Consequently, a large amount of digestate is generated which is regarded as a valuable soil amendment because it is rich in readily available macro- and micro- nutrients (Khoshnevisan et al., 2018b; Vaneeckhaute et al., 2013). However, the storage of such huge amount of digestate for a period of 3-6 months before field application would be problematic. Moreover,

the use of digestate as a substitution for chemical fertilizers results in significant costs for farmers because of its large volume and low dry matter content (Al Seadi et al., 2013). Furthermore, storage, transport, and application of digestate would bring about CH4 and NH3 emissions; the former contributes to global warming effect, and the latter causes eutrophication, and soil acidification (Longhurst et al., 2019; Styles et al., 2018). Besides, the oversupply of digestate in regions with in- tensive biogas plants, improper distribution of digestate on farmlands, as well as its application at improper time increase air and water pol- lutions (Fedorniak, 2017; Tsapekos et al., 2019). Accordingly, attempts have been made to introduce modern digestate treatment technologies to help better handle digestate while simultaneously decrease the ne- gative environmental impacts associated with its direct application (Rehl and Müller, 2011).

A wide range of technologies have been proposed and tested for digestate conditioning and treatment including solid-liquid separation (Vázquez-Rowe et al., 2015), composting (Zeng et al., 2016), drying (Vázquez-Rowe et al., 2015), ammonia stripping (Menkveld and Broeders, 2017), membrane filtration (Gienau et al., 2018), electro- coagulation (Mores et al., 2016), acidification and alkaline stabilization (Vaneeckhaute et al., 2017), algal nutrient-stripping (Xu et al., 2015), and anammox process (Al Seadi et al., 2013). Although various kinds of treatment technologies have been successfully tested, showing pro- mising progress for handling digestate, their environmental impacts are still unknown. Few studies have already scrutinized different digestate treatment technologies from environmental performance point of views (Collet et al., 2011), while some of them limited their system bound- aries and excluded up-stream and AD processes from their system boundaries such as Styles et al. (2018) and Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2015). Moreover, previous studies mostly focused on the digestate generated from lab-scale AD process of food waste, energy crops, and municipal waste (Rehl and Müller, 2011; Tampio et al., 2016), leaving a gap for manure-based digestate produced from large-scale biogas plant.

We have recently successfully tested a pilot reactor, working based on flocculation-biological contact oxidation method, as an innovative approach to decrease chemical oxygen demand and turbidity of PM digestate and make it a suitable media for microalgae cultivation (Zhou et al., 2019). A new technology, despite having a positive influence in one aspect, may impose negative impacts in other aspects making it difficult to find optimal compromises (Cleary, 2009; Rehl and Müller, 2011). Therefore, it is necessary to also appraise the environmental performance even at the early stages to obtain a holistic view point about the technology under consideration. Accordingly, the pros and cons are discussed at this step, the hotspots are introduced, and the newly introduced technology is usually compared with a baseline op- tion to see if there are any rooms for future improvements. Accordingly, the primary objective of the present study was to compare some al- ternative digestate treatment technologies from environmental point of views. Life cycle assessment (LCA) along with mass and energy balance was used in the present study to support policy makers through se- lecting the best available technologies with higher net energy balance and lower environmental impacts. The results were accompanied by a series of sensitivity analysis to examine how the output results would change in response to the variations in system parameters.

7: Currently, the produced digestate in the Chinese biogas plants, treating PM from large- and medium-scale pig farms, is directly used as a soil conditioner followed a 3 to 6-month storage period, providing nutrients to the soil and increasing soil organic matter. Accordingly, the direct use of digestate on farmlands was considered as the baseline scenario (Sc-1) in the present study to which other digestate treatment technologies were compared. Accordingly, in baseline scenario pig manure is undergone anaerobic digestion and the produced biogas is used as home cooking fuel to substitute for natural gas. The produced digesate is spread on the farmlands to substitute for chemical fertilizers.

In the second scenario, the produced biogas is partly combusted in a CHP plant to supply heat and electricity demand of whole biorefinery. The remaining biogas is used as home cooking fuel to substitute for natural gas. The produced digestate is mechanically separated into solid and liquid fractions. The solid fraction is then composted and applied as a soil conditioner. The liquid fraction is used as an enriched cultivation medium to grow microalgae, i.e., Chlorella 1067 (Deng et al., 2017). The liquid fraction, remaining after mechanical separation, would have high turbidity and high chromaticity which hinders and weakens the microalgae growth (Wang et al., 2010). Therefore, in the second sce- nario integrated flocculation-biological contact oxidation (IFBCO) technology as suggested by Zhou et al. (2019) was employed to remove organic matter and decrease turbidity and consequently provide a sui- table media for microalgae cultivation. The produced microalgae were then co-digested with PM to improve the energy recovery from PM (Wang et al., 2016). However, the successful performance of IFBCO depends on the consumption of flocculants and operating conditions. Thus, in the third scenario IFBCO was replaced with dilution (DL) method as promoted by Collet et al. (2011) for scaled-up microalgae cultivation. Accordingly, in the third scenario biogas was partly com- busted to supply biorefinery's heat and electricity demand. The surplus electricity is sold to the grid. The surplus biogas is used as home cooking fuel. The solid fraction of digestate was composted and used as soil conditioner while the liquid fraction was diluted to reach accep- table turbidity and ammonia level suitable for microalgae cultivation.

In the fourth scenario, the upcycling of the liquid fraction of di- gestate into powder biofertilizers (PBF) was investigated. Struvite pre- cipitation with ammonia stripping is an established technology to re- cover phosphorous and ammonia from aqueous residual streams such as biogas slurries (Vaneeckhaute et al., 2017). The extracted PBF is a concentrated biofertilizer which decrease logistical problems for its transportation and application on farmlands (Nkoa, 2014). Thus, this technology was selected as an alternative and compared with other developed scenarios. Similar to IFBCO and DL scenarios, internal heat and electricity demand was supplied via biogas combustion in a CHP plant while surplus biogas was assumed to substitute for natural gas. Table 1 summarizes the selected technologies and shows the differences among them. More detailed description and information will be pro- vided later on in the subsequent sub-sections.
Table 1 Scenario details created for treating pig manure digestate.

Scenario number|Scenario name|Description Sc-1|Baseline|Direct use of digestate on farmlands Sc-2|IFBCO|Composting solid fraction of digestate - Pretreatment of liquid fraction of digestate with integrated flocculation-biological contact oxidation and using it as medium for microalgae cultivation Sc-3|DL|Composting solid fraction of digestate - Diluting liquid fraction of digestate with water and using it as medium for microalgae cultivation Sc-4|PBF|Composting solid fraction of digestate - powder biofertilizers production via struvite precipitation with ammonia stripping

Scenario number|Scenario name|Description Sc-1|Baseline|Direct use of digestate on farmlands Sc-2|IFBCO|Composting solid fraction of digestate - Pretreatment of liquid fraction of digestate with integrated flocculation-biological contact oxidation and using it as medium for microalgae cultivation Sc-3|DL|Composting solid fraction of digestate - Diluting liquid fraction of digestate with water and using it as medium for microalgae cultivation Sc-4|PBF|Composting solid fraction of digestate - powder biofertilizers production via struvite precipitation with ammonia stripping
9: Currently, the produced digestate in the Chinese biogas plants, treating PM from large- and medium-scale pig farms, is directly used as a soil conditioner followed a 3 to 6-month storage period, providing nutrients to the soil and increasing soil organic matter. Accordingly, the direct use of digestate on farmlands was considered as the baseline scenario (Sc-1) in the present study to which other digestate treatment technologies were compared. Accordingly, in baseline scenario pig manure is undergone anaerobic digestion and the produced biogas is used as home cooking fuel to substitute for natural gas. The produced digesate is spread on the farmlands to substitute for chemical fertilizers.

In the second scenario, the produced biogas is partly combusted in a CHP plant to supply heat and electricity demand of whole biorefinery. The remaining biogas is used as home cooking fuel to substitute for natural gas. The produced digestate is mechanically separated into solid and liquid fractions. The solid fraction is then composted and applied as a soil conditioner. The liquid fraction is used as an enriched cultivation medium to grow microalgae, i.e., Chlorella 1067 (Deng et al., 2017). The liquid fraction, remaining after mechanical separation, would have high turbidity and high chromaticity which hinders and weakens the microalgae growth (Wang et al., 2010). Therefore, in the second sce- nario integrated flocculation-biological contact oxidation (IFBCO) technology as suggested by Zhou et al. (2019) was employed to remove organic matter and decrease turbidity and consequently provide a sui- table media for microalgae cultivation. The produced microalgae were then co-digested with PM to improve the energy recovery from PM (Wang et al., 2016). However, the successful performance of IFBCO depends on the consumption of flocculants and operating conditions. Thus, in the third scenario IFBCO was replaced with dilution (DL) method as promoted by Collet et al. (2011) for scaled-up microalgae cultivation. Accordingly, in the third scenario biogas was partly com- busted to supply biorefinery's heat and electricity demand. The surplus electricity is sold to the grid. The surplus biogas is used as home cooking fuel. The solid fraction of digestate was composted and used as soil conditioner while the liquid fraction was diluted to reach accep- table turbidity and ammonia level suitable for microalgae cultivation.

In the fourth scenario, the upcycling of the liquid fraction of di- gestate into powder biofertilizers (PBF) was investigated. Struvite pre- cipitation with ammonia stripping is an established technology to re- cover phosphorous and ammonia from aqueous residual streams such as biogas slurries (Vaneeckhaute et al., 2017). The extracted PBF is a concentrated biofertilizer which decrease logistical problems for its transportation and application on farmlands (Nkoa, 2014). Thus, this technology was selected as an alternative and compared with other developed scenarios. Similar to IFBCO and DL scenarios, internal heat and electricity demand was supplied via biogas combustion in a CHP plant while surplus biogas was assumed to substitute for natural gas. Table 1 summarizes the selected technologies and shows the differences among them. More detailed description and information will be pro- vided later on in the subsequent sub-sections.
Table 1 Scenario details created for treating pig manure digestate.

11: The LCA framework and methodology used here comply with ISO 14,040 guidelines (ISO, 2006) and recommendations presented by Re- ference Life Cycle Data System (ILCD) Handbook (Wolf et al., 2010). According to the guidelines presented by ILCD, this study would be a change-oriented LCA and fall into meso/macro-level category which has been described as situation B. This implies that scenarios under consideration should be modeled with long-term marginal data.

The main goal of the present study was to assess the potential en- vironmental impacts of some alternative digestate treatment technolo- gies - briefly described under sub-section 2.1 - in order to handle di- gestate from biogas plants in China. Due to the fact that developed scenarios would have different water requirements and also can deliver process water for agro-industrial consumption, water footprint was also included in the scope of the present study.

The above-mentioned processing options would have structural in- fluence on the down- and up-stream processes. For instance, compost, digestate, and PBF differ in terms of air emissions when they are applied on farms. Furthermore, co-digestion of PM and microalgae affects carbon and nutrient cycles. Thereon, the system boundary opted herein was expanded to cover not only digestate processing technologies but also the down- and up-stream processes. Such an approach differ- entiates the present research from previously conducted studies whose focus was only kept on the digestate processing step (Rehl and Müller, 2011; Styles et al., 2018; Vázquez-Rowe et al., 2015). Accordingly, the system boundary comprised the transportation of PM to AD plants, biogas production, combustion of biogas to generate power/combined heat and power to meet plants' internal demands, subsequent digestate storage and treatment options, transportation as well as use of diges- tate/compost/PBF on farmlands, cultivation of microalgae and its re- lated processing steps, and finally substitution of biogas and bio- fertilizers for their counterparts from fossil resources. Figs. 1-4 illustrate mass flows through the systems.

The functional unit (FU) was defined as 1 metric tonne of PM which entered to the system boundary. The PM used in this research work was collected from pig farms near the Xibaidian biogas plant (Pinggu, Beijing, China). The characteristics of PM are summarized in Table 2.
Detailed information concerning all processes and assumptions used to constitute the model are described in the following subsections.

This process refers to the mono- and co-digestion of PM. The in- ventory data related to the mono-digestion of PM was collected from Xibaidian biogas plant. The biogas plant has an up-flow solid reactor (USR) with a total volume of 600 m³. Detailed data about this biogas plant is presented in supporting information (Table S1 and S2). The average monthly PM, anaerobically digested in this biogas plant, was 133 ( + 19) tonne. The mean biogas yield was calculated from the plant's biogas production data recorded between Feb-2015 to Jan-2017. Accordingly, the average biogas yield was estimated at 420 ( + 104) L/ kgVS with an average methane content of 65%. Regarding the co-di- gestion of PM and microalgae, theoretical calculations were performed based on our previous lab-scale experiments (Li et al., 2017) as well as data from literature review. The previous lab-scale studies unanimously proved that the co-digestion of microalgae would improve biogas yield from biological degradation of waste activated sludge (Beltrán et al., 2016), PM (Wang et al., 2016), and chicken manure (Li et al., 2017). Wang et al. (2016) found that microalgae could improve the biogas production from PM up to 11.2% when microalgae constituted less than 6% of the total Substrate Accordingly, the biogas yield from co-di- gestion of microalgae and PM was estimated at 453 L/kgVS. The energy demand of the biogas plant was composed of the elec- tricity consumption for the heating system (i.e., air heat pumps), mixers, pumps, gas compressor, etc. It should be highlighted that in "Xibaidian biogas plant", the thermal energy demand is supplied via air heat pumps, thus electricity is the sole form of energy consumed in the plant. Accordingly, it was assumed that the electricity was internally supplied by a biogas engine combusting a fraction of produced biogas. An electrical efficiency of 42% of the biogas lower heating value (LHV) was considered in this context (Khoshnevisan et al., 2018b; Naroznova et al., 2016). Having considered that the total energy efficiency of modern combined heat and power (CHP) engines exceeds 80% (Moller et al., 2009), sole electricity production via biogas engines would result in losing part of the biogas energy content. Therefore, CHP engines with electrical and thermal efficiency of 39% and 46%, respectively, were considered to supply plant's energy demand for scenario 2 to 4. The
"Chemical fertilizer Prod."|9.43 kg Urea, 5 kg P2O5, 6.97 kg K2O "Chemical fertilizer Prod."| "Natural gas extraction"|26.81 kg natural gas "Natural gas extraction"|
Fig. 1. Mass flow of 1 tonne of pig manure and its associated products and by-products under baseline scenario. The gray dotted boxes and lines show the materials which are substituted.
Fig. 2. Mass flow of 1 tonne of pig manure and its associated products and by-products under IFBCO. The gray dotted boxes and lines show the materials which are
"Chemical fertilizer Prod."| "Chemical fertilizer Prod."|2.35 kg Urea, 5.02 kg P2O5, 0.74 kg K2O "Natural gas extraction"| "Natural gas extraction"|28.59 kg natural gas
emission factors, shown in Table 3, was used to compile inventory data related to the air emissions from biogas engines and CHP units.

A fraction of produced biogas is lost through leaking to the air which contributes to global warming and climate change. Due to the fact that no actual measurement was done to determine the biogas leakage, data from literature was employed. According to Hjort- Gregersen (2013) and Naroznova et al. (2016) almost 2.7% of the produced CH4 in the biogas plants is lost through the leakage to air.

Seasonal samples were taken from the produced digestate in the biogas plant to measure critical parameters such as pH, COD, TAN, turbidity, etc., required to perform a mass balance. The pH, COD, TP, NH3-N and turbidity were measured according to the APHA standard methods (Apha, 1985).

It is worth mentioning that the production of capital equipment such as constructions and machinery were excluded from the inventory analysis of the present study. This is in line with the previous studies in which capital equipment were excluded due to their long life time (Khoshnevisan et al., 2020; Rehl and Müller, 2011; Vázquez-Rowe et al., 2015). Moreover, some studies in which capital equipment is included in the inventory analysis unanimously came into the conclu- sion that they would not contribute to the environmental footprints of AD related processes and their final products (Starr et al., 2012).

According to the guidelines presented by ILCD, this study would be a change-oriented LCA and fall into meso/macro-level category which has been described as situation B. This implies that scenarios under consideration should be modeled with long-term marginal data.

Having considered that the total energy efficiency of modern combined heat and power (CHP) engines exceeds 80% (Moller et al., 2009), sole electricity production via biogas engines would result in losing part of the biogas energy content. Therefore, CHP engines with electrical and thermal efficiency of 39% and 46%, respectively, were considered to supply plant's energy demand for scenario 2 to 4. The

This implies that scenarios under consideration should be modeled with long-term marginal data.

The main goal of the present study was to assess the potential en- vironmental impacts of some alternative digestate treatment technolo- gies - briefly described under sub-section 2.1 - in order to handle di- gestate from biogas plants in China. Due to the fact that developed scenarios would have different water requirements and also can deliver process water for agro-industrial consumption, water footprint was also included in the scope of the present study.

The above-mentioned processing options would have structural in- fluence on the down- and up-stream processes. For instance, compost, digestate, and PBF differ in terms of air emissions when they are applied on farms. Furthermore, co-digestion of PM and microalgae affects carbon and nutrient cycles. Thereon, the system boundary opted herein was expanded to cover not only digestate processing technologies but also the down- and up-stream processes. Such an approach differ- entiates the present research from previously conducted studies whose focus was only kept on the digestate processing step (Rehl and Müller, 2011; Styles et al., 2018; Vázquez-Rowe et al., 2015). Accordingly, the system boundary comprised the transportation of PM to AD plants, biogas production, combustion of biogas to generate power/combined heat and power to meet plants' internal demands, subsequent digestate storage and treatment options, transportation as well as use of diges- tate/compost/PBF on farmlands, cultivation of microalgae and its re- lated processing steps, and finally substitution of biogas and bio- fertilizers for their counterparts from fossil resources. Figs. 1-4 illustrate mass flows through the systems.

The functional unit (FU) was defined as 1 metric tonne of PM which entered to the system boundary. The PM used in this research work was collected from pig farms near the Xibaidian biogas plant (Pinggu, Beijing, China). The characteristics of PM are summarized in Table 2.
Detailed information concerning all processes and assumptions used to constitute the model are described in the following subsections.

This process refers to the mono- and co-digestion of PM. The in- ventory data related to the mono-digestion of PM was collected from Xibaidian biogas plant. The biogas plant has an up-flow solid reactor (USR) with a total volume of 600 m³. Detailed data about this biogas plant is presented in supporting information (Table S1 and S2). The average monthly PM, anaerobically digested in this biogas plant, was 133 ( + 19) tonne. The mean biogas yield was calculated from the plant's biogas production data recorded between Feb-2015 to Jan-2017. Accordingly, the average biogas yield was estimated at 420 ( + 104) L/ kgVS with an average methane content of 65%. Regarding the co-di- gestion of PM and microalgae, theoretical calculations were performed based on our previous lab-scale experiments (Li et al., 2017) as well as data from literature review. The previous lab-scale studies unanimously proved that the co-digestion of microalgae would improve biogas yield from biological degradation of waste activated sludge (Beltrán et al., 2016), PM (Wang et al., 2016), and chicken manure (Li et al., 2017). Wang et al. (2016) found that microalgae could improve the biogas production from PM up to 11.2% when microalgae constituted less than 6% of the total Substrate Accordingly, the biogas yield from co-di- gestion of microalgae and PM was estimated at 453 L/kgVS. The energy demand of the biogas plant was composed of the elec- tricity consumption for the heating system (i.e., air heat pumps), mixers, pumps, gas compressor, etc. It should be highlighted that in "Xibaidian biogas plant", the thermal energy demand is supplied via air heat pumps, thus electricity is the sole form of energy consumed in the plant. Accordingly, it was assumed that the electricity was internally supplied by a biogas engine combusting a fraction of produced biogas. An electrical efficiency of 42% of the biogas lower heating value (LHV) was considered in this context (Khoshnevisan et al., 2018b; Naroznova et al., 2016). Having considered that the total energy efficiency of modern combined heat and power (CHP) engines exceeds 80% (Moller et al., 2009), sole electricity production via biogas engines would result in losing part of the biogas energy content. Therefore, CHP engines with electrical and thermal efficiency of 39% and 46%, respectively, were considered to supply plant's energy demand for scenario 2 to 4. The

19: Digestate produced from AD process needs to be stored for a period and then used on farmlands during cultivation season. This period varies depending on the cultivation varieties. Digestate, leaving the anaerobic reactors, is still active SO continues producing methane.

Furthermore, ammonia is also emitted from digestate during its storage before field application. The amount of methane and ammonia emis- sions during the storage period highly depends on the type of storage system which can be sealed tanks, covered tanks, open tanks, or lagoons (IPCC, 2006). The produced digestate in "Xibaidian biogas plant" was stored in an open tank for 3-6 months before being applied to the land, SO the same storage system was also considered for scenario 2 to 4. The methane emissions from storage systems were calculated as shown in Table 4. It should be heighted that the digestate treatment technologies would shorten the storage period since they can be directly sent to the post-treatment plant and consequently decrease the subsequent emis- sions.

The type of storage system also determines the amount of ammonia emissions. Ammonia emission factor applies only to the ammonium nitrogen in the liquid digestate. Accordingly, ammonia emission factor for open tanks, covered tanks, and sealed tanks was considered to be 10%, 5%, and 2%, respectively (Styles et al., 2016). N2O emissions were not included because Rodhe et al. (2015) showed that N2O emissions during storage period were negligible even under open storage system. A sensitivity analysis was also applied to investigate the type of di- gestate storage on the overall results.

Solid-liquid separation aims at partitioning digestate into a phos- phorous-rich high dry matter solid fraction and a nitrogen-rich low dry matter liquid fraction (Al Seadi et al., 2013). A variety of technologies have been reportedly tested such as decanter centrifuge, screw press, and sieve belt. Among the technologies, available on the market,

"Chemical fertilizer Prod."| "Chemical fertilizer Prod."|2.85 kg Urea, 5.20 kg P2O5, 1.96 kg K2O "Natural gas extraction"|2.85 kg Urea, 5.20 kg P2O5, 1.96 kg K2O "Natural gas extraction"|4.38 kg natural gas
Fig. 3. Mass flow of 1 tonne of pig manure and its associated products and by-products under DL. The gray dotted boxes and lines show the materials which are substituted.

decanter centrifuge has gained popularity and is widely applied in manure co-digestion plants (Al Seadi et al., 2013). Therefore, it was chosen for the present study to be used for solid-liquid separation. Nutrient partitioning between solid and liquid fractions was performed according to the partitioning factors presented in Table 5. The elec- tricity demand of the decanter centrifuge was 4 kWh/m³.
2.3.4. Composting the solid fraction of digestate

The inventory data for composting process covered energy con- sumption and the subsequent air emissions during aerobic degradation of materials. According to Rehl and Müller (2011), 8.12% of total carbon, 20% of NH4-N, and 1.4% of the total nitrogen are considered as CH4, NH3-N, and N2O-N emissions, respectively. The associated in- ventory data for composting process are summarized in Table 6.

IFBCO technology consisted of a flocculation pool (280 L), a sedi- mentation pool (1040 L), an aeration pool (3600 L), as well as dosing pools (280L). An anoxic section was added before the aerobic section to improve the process efficiency. Detailed information can be found in our previous study Zhou et al. (2019). The inventory data associated with IFBCO technology was collected from our pilot reactor located in Xibaidian biogas plant (Pinggu, Beijing, China). The collected data composed of polymeric aluminum chlorhydrate (PAC) and anionic po- lyacrylamide (APAM) consumption rate and energy demand. PAC and APAM consumption rate as well as electricity consumption were esti- mated at 1.2 g/L, 2 mg/L and 1.98 kWh/m³, respectively. The

flocculated organic materials, leaving IFBCO process, contained nu- trients, SO it was assumed to be used as soil conditioner to replace chemical fertilizers. Moreover, they contained organic carbons con- tributing to soil organic matter. The liquid fraction remained after IFBCO process was used for microalgae cultivation. The background data associated with chemical production was taken from ecoinvent database V3.3.

As discussed earlier, dilution is a method which can be employed to decrease biogas slurry's turbidity, and provide a proper media to grow microalgae. In order to increase the efficiency of the method and de- crease water consumption, a natural settling process followed by a centrifugation step was considered as a pretreatment method. In other words, the liquid fraction of digestate leaving decanter centrifuge was directed to a settling tank. The naturally settled organic materials contained N (1.9 %TS), P (5 %TS), K (1.8 %TS), and C (87.87 %TS), SO it was assumed to be used on farmlands. The supernatant was then centrifuged to remove more organic matters. The remaining liquid after centrifugation was diluted with water to preferred TAN concentration, i.e., (0.3 g/L), suitable for microalgae cultivation, as shown in our previous study by Zhou et al. (2019). The electricity consumption for centrifugation step was estimated at 0.04 kWh/L (Collet et al., 2011). Accordingly, a total energy demand of 143.07 kWh/FU was calculated.
The potential of microalgae cultivation using liquid fraction of PM digestate was based on our previously lab-scale microalgae cultivation

as reported in Zhou et al. (2019). Based on the lab-scale results as well as figures derived from literature, extrapolation was performed to de- termine microalgae yields and nutrient removal under scaled-up op- erations. The hypothetical microalgae cultivation system was open ra- ceways because mass production of microalgae in such cultivation system is more appropriate than photobioreactors (Chisti, 2007). Due to

the fact that the available macro- and micro-nutrient in the liquid fraction of PM digestate were sufficient to grow microalgae (based on results from lab-scale experiments), no additional nutrients were con- sidered for scaled-up cultivation. The inventory data for microalgae cultivation comprised of CO2 consumption, electricity consumption for CO2 injection as well as for paddlewheels to move water in the system

20: Digestate produced from AD process needs to be stored for a period and then used on farmlands during cultivation season. This period varies depending on the cultivation varieties. Digestate, leaving the anaerobic reactors, is still active SO continues producing methane.

Furthermore, ammonia is also emitted from digestate during its storage before field application. The amount of methane and ammonia emis- sions during the storage period highly depends on the type of storage system which can be sealed tanks, covered tanks, open tanks, or lagoons (IPCC, 2006). The produced digestate in "Xibaidian biogas plant" was stored in an open tank for 3-6 months before being applied to the land, SO the same storage system was also considered for scenario 2 to 4. The methane emissions from storage systems were calculated as shown in Table 4. It should be heighted that the digestate treatment technologies would shorten the storage period since they can be directly sent to the post-treatment plant and consequently decrease the subsequent emis- sions.

The type of storage system also determines the amount of ammonia emissions. Ammonia emission factor applies only to the ammonium nitrogen in the liquid digestate. Accordingly, ammonia emission factor for open tanks, covered tanks, and sealed tanks was considered to be 10%, 5%, and 2%, respectively (Styles et al., 2016). N2O emissions were not included because Rodhe et al. (2015) showed that N2O emissions during storage period were negligible even under open storage system. A sensitivity analysis was also applied to investigate the type of di- gestate storage on the overall results.

Solid-liquid separation aims at partitioning digestate into a phos- phorous-rich high dry matter solid fraction and a nitrogen-rich low dry matter liquid fraction (Al Seadi et al., 2013). A variety of technologies have been reportedly tested such as decanter centrifuge, screw press, and sieve belt. Among the technologies, available on the market,

"Chemical fertilizer Prod."| "Chemical fertilizer Prod."|2.85 kg Urea, 5.20 kg P2O5, 1.96 kg K2O "Natural gas extraction"|2.85 kg Urea, 5.20 kg P2O5, 1.96 kg K2O "Natural gas extraction"|4.38 kg natural gas
Fig. 3. Mass flow of 1 tonne of pig manure and its associated products and by-products under DL. The gray dotted boxes and lines show the materials which are substituted.

decanter centrifuge has gained popularity and is widely applied in manure co-digestion plants (Al Seadi et al., 2013). Therefore, it was chosen for the present study to be used for solid-liquid separation. Nutrient partitioning between solid and liquid fractions was performed according to the partitioning factors presented in Table 5. The elec- tricity demand of the decanter centrifuge was 4 kWh/m³.

23: Digestate produced from AD process needs to be stored for a period and then used on farmlands during cultivation season. This period varies depending on the cultivation varieties. Digestate, leaving the anaerobic reactors, is still active SO continues producing methane.

Furthermore, ammonia is also emitted from digestate during its storage before field application. The amount of methane and ammonia emis- sions during the storage period highly depends on the type of storage system which can be sealed tanks, covered tanks, open tanks, or lagoons (IPCC, 2006). The produced digestate in "Xibaidian biogas plant" was stored in an open tank for 3-6 months before being applied to the land, SO the same storage system was also considered for scenario 2 to 4. The methane emissions from storage systems were calculated as shown in Table 4. It should be heighted that the digestate treatment technologies would shorten the storage period since they can be directly sent to the post-treatment plant and consequently decrease the subsequent emis- sions.

The type of storage system also determines the amount of ammonia emissions. Ammonia emission factor applies only to the ammonium nitrogen in the liquid digestate. Accordingly, ammonia emission factor for open tanks, covered tanks, and sealed tanks was considered to be 10%, 5%, and 2%, respectively (Styles et al., 2016). N2O emissions were not included because Rodhe et al. (2015) showed that N2O emissions during storage period were negligible even under open storage system. A sensitivity analysis was also applied to investigate the type of di- gestate storage on the overall results.

Solid-liquid separation aims at partitioning digestate into a phos- phorous-rich high dry matter solid fraction and a nitrogen-rich low dry matter liquid fraction (Al Seadi et al., 2013). A variety of technologies have been reportedly tested such as decanter centrifuge, screw press, and sieve belt. Among the technologies, available on the market,

"Chemical fertilizer Prod."| "Chemical fertilizer Prod."|2.85 kg Urea, 5.20 kg P2O5, 1.96 kg K2O "Natural gas extraction"|2.85 kg Urea, 5.20 kg P2O5, 1.96 kg K2O "Natural gas extraction"|4.38 kg natural gas
Fig. 3. Mass flow of 1 tonne of pig manure and its associated products and by-products under DL. The gray dotted boxes and lines show the materials which are substituted.

decanter centrifuge has gained popularity and is widely applied in manure co-digestion plants (Al Seadi et al., 2013). Therefore, it was chosen for the present study to be used for solid-liquid separation. Nutrient partitioning between solid and liquid fractions was performed according to the partitioning factors presented in Table 5. The elec- tricity demand of the decanter centrifuge was 4 kWh/m³.
2.3.4. Composting the solid fraction of digestate

The inventory data for composting process covered energy con- sumption and the subsequent air emissions during aerobic degradation of materials. According to Rehl and Müller (2011), 8.12% of total carbon, 20% of NH4-N, and 1.4% of the total nitrogen are considered as CH4, NH3-N, and N2O-N emissions, respectively. The associated in- ventory data for composting process are summarized in Table 6.

IFBCO technology consisted of a flocculation pool (280 L), a sedi- mentation pool (1040 L), an aeration pool (3600 L), as well as dosing pools (280L). An anoxic section was added before the aerobic section to improve the process efficiency. Detailed information can be found in our previous study Zhou et al. (2019). The inventory data associated with IFBCO technology was collected from our pilot reactor located in Xibaidian biogas plant (Pinggu, Beijing, China). The collected data composed of polymeric aluminum chlorhydrate (PAC) and anionic po- lyacrylamide (APAM) consumption rate and energy demand. PAC and APAM consumption rate as well as electricity consumption were esti- mated at 1.2 g/L, 2 mg/L and 1.98 kWh/m³, respectively. The

flocculated organic materials, leaving IFBCO process, contained nu- trients, SO it was assumed to be used as soil conditioner to replace chemical fertilizers. Moreover, they contained organic carbons con- tributing to soil organic matter. The liquid fraction remained after IFBCO process was used for microalgae cultivation. The background data associated with chemical production was taken from ecoinvent database V3.3.

As discussed earlier, dilution is a method which can be employed to decrease biogas slurry's turbidity, and provide a proper media to grow microalgae. In order to increase the efficiency of the method and de- crease water consumption, a natural settling process followed by a centrifugation step was considered as a pretreatment method. In other words, the liquid fraction of digestate leaving decanter centrifuge was directed to a settling tank. The naturally settled organic materials contained N (1.9 %TS), P (5 %TS), K (1.8 %TS), and C (87.87 %TS), SO it was assumed to be used on farmlands. The supernatant was then centrifuged to remove more organic matters. The remaining liquid after centrifugation was diluted with water to preferred TAN concentration, i.e., (0.3 g/L), suitable for microalgae cultivation, as shown in our previous study by Zhou et al. (2019). The electricity consumption for centrifugation step was estimated at 0.04 kWh/L (Collet et al., 2011). Accordingly, a total energy demand of 143.07 kWh/FU was calculated.
The potential of microalgae cultivation using liquid fraction of PM digestate was based on our previously lab-scale microalgae cultivation

26: Digestate produced from AD process needs to be stored for a period and then used on farmlands during cultivation season. This period varies depending on the cultivation varieties. Digestate, leaving the anaerobic reactors, is still active SO continues producing methane.

Furthermore, ammonia is also emitted from digestate during its storage before field application. The amount of methane and ammonia emis- sions during the storage period highly depends on the type of storage system which can be sealed tanks, covered tanks, open tanks, or lagoons (IPCC, 2006). The produced digestate in "Xibaidian biogas plant" was stored in an open tank for 3-6 months before being applied to the land, SO the same storage system was also considered for scenario 2 to 4. The methane emissions from storage systems were calculated as shown in Table 4. It should be heighted that the digestate treatment technologies would shorten the storage period since they can be directly sent to the post-treatment plant and consequently decrease the subsequent emis- sions.

The type of storage system also determines the amount of ammonia emissions. Ammonia emission factor applies only to the ammonium nitrogen in the liquid digestate. Accordingly, ammonia emission factor for open tanks, covered tanks, and sealed tanks was considered to be 10%, 5%, and 2%, respectively (Styles et al., 2016). N2O emissions were not included because Rodhe et al. (2015) showed that N2O emissions during storage period were negligible even under open storage system. A sensitivity analysis was also applied to investigate the type of di- gestate storage on the overall results.

Solid-liquid separation aims at partitioning digestate into a phos- phorous-rich high dry matter solid fraction and a nitrogen-rich low dry matter liquid fraction (Al Seadi et al., 2013). A variety of technologies have been reportedly tested such as decanter centrifuge, screw press, and sieve belt. Among the technologies, available on the market,

"Chemical fertilizer Prod."| "Chemical fertilizer Prod."|2.85 kg Urea, 5.20 kg P2O5, 1.96 kg K2O "Natural gas extraction"|2.85 kg Urea, 5.20 kg P2O5, 1.96 kg K2O "Natural gas extraction"|4.38 kg natural gas
Fig. 3. Mass flow of 1 tonne of pig manure and its associated products and by-products under DL. The gray dotted boxes and lines show the materials which are substituted.

decanter centrifuge has gained popularity and is widely applied in manure co-digestion plants (Al Seadi et al., 2013). Therefore, it was chosen for the present study to be used for solid-liquid separation. Nutrient partitioning between solid and liquid fractions was performed according to the partitioning factors presented in Table 5. The elec- tricity demand of the decanter centrifuge was 4 kWh/m³.
2.3.4. Composting the solid fraction of digestate

The inventory data for composting process covered energy con- sumption and the subsequent air emissions during aerobic degradation of materials. According to Rehl and Müller (2011), 8.12% of total carbon, 20% of NH4-N, and 1.4% of the total nitrogen are considered as CH4, NH3-N, and N2O-N emissions, respectively. The associated in- ventory data for composting process are summarized in Table 6.

IFBCO technology consisted of a flocculation pool (280 L), a sedi- mentation pool (1040 L), an aeration pool (3600 L), as well as dosing pools (280L). An anoxic section was added before the aerobic section to improve the process efficiency. Detailed information can be found in our previous study Zhou et al. (2019). The inventory data associated with IFBCO technology was collected from our pilot reactor located in Xibaidian biogas plant (Pinggu, Beijing, China). The collected data composed of polymeric aluminum chlorhydrate (PAC) and anionic po- lyacrylamide (APAM) consumption rate and energy demand. PAC and APAM consumption rate as well as electricity consumption were esti- mated at 1.2 g/L, 2 mg/L and 1.98 kWh/m³, respectively. The

flocculated organic materials, leaving IFBCO process, contained nu- trients, SO it was assumed to be used as soil conditioner to replace chemical fertilizers. Moreover, they contained organic carbons con- tributing to soil organic matter. The liquid fraction remained after IFBCO process was used for microalgae cultivation. The background data associated with chemical production was taken from ecoinvent database V3.3.

As discussed earlier, dilution is a method which can be employed to decrease biogas slurry's turbidity, and provide a proper media to grow microalgae. In order to increase the efficiency of the method and de- crease water consumption, a natural settling process followed by a centrifugation step was considered as a pretreatment method. In other words, the liquid fraction of digestate leaving decanter centrifuge was directed to a settling tank. The naturally settled organic materials contained N (1.9 %TS), P (5 %TS), K (1.8 %TS), and C (87.87 %TS), SO it was assumed to be used on farmlands. The supernatant was then centrifuged to remove more organic matters. The remaining liquid after centrifugation was diluted with water to preferred TAN concentration, i.e., (0.3 g/L), suitable for microalgae cultivation, as shown in our previous study by Zhou et al. (2019). The electricity consumption for centrifugation step was estimated at 0.04 kWh/L (Collet et al., 2011). Accordingly, a total energy demand of 143.07 kWh/FU was calculated.
The potential of microalgae cultivation using liquid fraction of PM digestate was based on our previously lab-scale microalgae cultivation
11.32 kg Urea, 4.89 kg P2O5, 0.54 kg K2O
Fig. 4. Mass flow of 1 tonne of pig manure and its associated products and by-products under PBF. The gray dotted boxes and lines show the materials which are substituted.

as reported in Zhou et al. (2019). Based on the lab-scale results as well as figures derived from literature, extrapolation was performed to de- termine microalgae yields and nutrient removal under scaled-up op- erations. The hypothetical microalgae cultivation system was open ra- ceways because mass production of microalgae in such cultivation system is more appropriate than photobioreactors (Chisti, 2007). Due to

the fact that the available macro- and micro-nutrient in the liquid fraction of PM digestate were sufficient to grow microalgae (based on results from lab-scale experiments), no additional nutrients were con- sidered for scaled-up cultivation. The inventory data for microalgae cultivation comprised of CO2 consumption, electricity consumption for CO2 injection as well as for paddlewheels to move water in the system

29: Digestate produced from AD process needs to be stored for a period and then used on farmlands during cultivation season. This period varies depending on the cultivation varieties. Digestate, leaving the anaerobic reactors, is still active SO continues producing methane.

Furthermore, ammonia is also emitted from digestate during its storage before field application. The amount of methane and ammonia emis- sions during the storage period highly depends on the type of storage system which can be sealed tanks, covered tanks, open tanks, or lagoons (IPCC, 2006). The produced digestate in "Xibaidian biogas plant" was stored in an open tank for 3-6 months before being applied to the land, SO the same storage system was also considered for scenario 2 to 4. The methane emissions from storage systems were calculated as shown in Table 4. It should be heighted that the digestate treatment technologies would shorten the storage period since they can be directly sent to the post-treatment plant and consequently decrease the subsequent emis- sions.

The type of storage system also determines the amount of ammonia emissions. Ammonia emission factor applies only to the ammonium nitrogen in the liquid digestate. Accordingly, ammonia emission factor for open tanks, covered tanks, and sealed tanks was considered to be 10%, 5%, and 2%, respectively (Styles et al., 2016). N2O emissions were not included because Rodhe et al. (2015) showed that N2O emissions during storage period were negligible even under open storage system. A sensitivity analysis was also applied to investigate the type of di- gestate storage on the overall results.

Solid-liquid separation aims at partitioning digestate into a phos- phorous-rich high dry matter solid fraction and a nitrogen-rich low dry matter liquid fraction (Al Seadi et al., 2013). A variety of technologies have been reportedly tested such as decanter centrifuge, screw press, and sieve belt. Among the technologies, available on the market,

"Chemical fertilizer Prod."| "Chemical fertilizer Prod."|2.85 kg Urea, 5.20 kg P2O5, 1.96 kg K2O "Natural gas extraction"|2.85 kg Urea, 5.20 kg P2O5, 1.96 kg K2O "Natural gas extraction"|4.38 kg natural gas
Fig. 3. Mass flow of 1 tonne of pig manure and its associated products and by-products under DL. The gray dotted boxes and lines show the materials which are substituted.

decanter centrifuge has gained popularity and is widely applied in manure co-digestion plants (Al Seadi et al., 2013). Therefore, it was chosen for the present study to be used for solid-liquid separation. Nutrient partitioning between solid and liquid fractions was performed according to the partitioning factors presented in Table 5. The elec- tricity demand of the decanter centrifuge was 4 kWh/m³.
2.3.4. Composting the solid fraction of digestate

The inventory data for composting process covered energy con- sumption and the subsequent air emissions during aerobic degradation of materials. According to Rehl and Müller (2011), 8.12% of total carbon, 20% of NH4-N, and 1.4% of the total nitrogen are considered as CH4, NH3-N, and N2O-N emissions, respectively. The associated in- ventory data for composting process are summarized in Table 6.

IFBCO technology consisted of a flocculation pool (280 L), a sedi- mentation pool (1040 L), an aeration pool (3600 L), as well as dosing pools (280L). An anoxic section was added before the aerobic section to improve the process efficiency. Detailed information can be found in our previous study Zhou et al. (2019). The inventory data associated with IFBCO technology was collected from our pilot reactor located in Xibaidian biogas plant (Pinggu, Beijing, China). The collected data composed of polymeric aluminum chlorhydrate (PAC) and anionic po- lyacrylamide (APAM) consumption rate and energy demand. PAC and APAM consumption rate as well as electricity consumption were esti- mated at 1.2 g/L, 2 mg/L and 1.98 kWh/m³, respectively. The

flocculated organic materials, leaving IFBCO process, contained nu- trients, SO it was assumed to be used as soil conditioner to replace chemical fertilizers. Moreover, they contained organic carbons con- tributing to soil organic matter. The liquid fraction remained after IFBCO process was used for microalgae cultivation. The background data associated with chemical production was taken from ecoinvent database V3.3.

As discussed earlier, dilution is a method which can be employed to decrease biogas slurry's turbidity, and provide a proper media to grow microalgae. In order to increase the efficiency of the method and de- crease water consumption, a natural settling process followed by a centrifugation step was considered as a pretreatment method. In other words, the liquid fraction of digestate leaving decanter centrifuge was directed to a settling tank. The naturally settled organic materials contained N (1.9 %TS), P (5 %TS), K (1.8 %TS), and C (87.87 %TS), SO it was assumed to be used on farmlands. The supernatant was then centrifuged to remove more organic matters. The remaining liquid after centrifugation was diluted with water to preferred TAN concentration, i.e., (0.3 g/L), suitable for microalgae cultivation, as shown in our previous study by Zhou et al. (2019). The electricity consumption for centrifugation step was estimated at 0.04 kWh/L (Collet et al., 2011). Accordingly, a total energy demand of 143.07 kWh/FU was calculated.
The potential of microalgae cultivation using liquid fraction of PM digestate was based on our previously lab-scale microalgae cultivation

as reported in Zhou et al. (2019). Based on the lab-scale results as well as figures derived from literature, extrapolation was performed to de- termine microalgae yields and nutrient removal under scaled-up op- erations. The hypothetical microalgae cultivation system was open ra- ceways because mass production of microalgae in such cultivation system is more appropriate than photobioreactors (Chisti, 2007). Due to

the fact that the available macro- and micro-nutrient in the liquid fraction of PM digestate were sufficient to grow microalgae (based on results from lab-scale experiments), no additional nutrients were con- sidered for scaled-up cultivation. 32: The produced biomass was then directed to the new settling tanks to harvest microalgae using natural settling. Harvested microalgae was supposed to be mixed with PM and co-digested in the anaerobic reactor. A fraction of liquid media remains after natural settling was used as process water to dilute PM before AD and the rest was assumed to be used as irrigation water in agriculture.
33: Struvite precipitation plus ammonia striping technology was con- sidered as another possibility for nutrient recovery from digestate. The potential of powder biofertilizer production was based on theoretical calculations, literature review, and technical reports (Hallas et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019). The following equation describes the reaction associated with struvite precipitation:
Mg2+ + NH4 + HPO42 + OH + 5 H2O - MgNH4PO4 6 H2O

In this process, magnesium chloride is used to precipitate magne- sium ammonium phosphate. In other words, magnesium ammonium phosphate was precipitated through addition of magnesium chloride and pH control by aeration and crystallized precipitation of P. At the next step ammonium sulfate is extracted and finally powder bio- fertilizer is produced. This process involves the air-stripping of am- monia from liquid digestate flowing down through a packed column, followed by crystallization recovery using sulfuric acid to produce solid ammonium sulfate. Under elevated temperature and controlled pH, higher efficiency would be achieved. The inventory data associated with struvite precipitation unit contains magnesium chloride and electricity consumption. The inventory data related to ammonium sulfate extraction included heat and electricity demand as well as the consumption of NaOH, H2SO4, and citric acid. All inventory data compiled for PBF production is tabulated in Table 8. The background data related to the production of chemicals was taken from ecoinvent database.
34: Struvite precipitation plus ammonia striping technology was con- sidered as another possibility for nutrient recovery from digestate. The potential of powder biofertilizer production was based on theoretical calculations, literature review, and technical reports (Hallas et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019). The following equation describes the reaction associated with struvite precipitation:
Mg2+ + NH4 + HPO42 + OH + 5 H2O - MgNH4PO4 6 H2O

In this process, magnesium chloride is used to precipitate magne- sium ammonium phosphate. In other words, magnesium ammonium phosphate was precipitated through addition of magnesium chloride and pH control by aeration and crystallized precipitation of P. At the next step ammonium sulfate is extracted and finally powder bio- fertilizer is produced. This process involves the air-stripping of am- monia from liquid digestate flowing down through a packed column, followed by crystallization recovery using sulfuric acid to produce solid ammonium sulfate. Under elevated temperature and controlled pH, higher efficiency would be achieved. The inventory data associated with struvite precipitation unit contains magnesium chloride and electricity consumption. The inventory data related to ammonium sulfate extraction included heat and electricity demand as well as the consumption of NaOH, H2SO4, and citric acid. All inventory data compiled for PBF production is tabulated in Table 8. The background data related to the production of chemicals was taken from ecoinvent database.

2.3.10. Application of digestate and other bio-fertilizers on farmlands

The use of chemical fertilizers, digestate, compost, PBF, and any other soil conditioner is subject to the emission of pollutants. Nitrogen containing fertilizers (both bio and chemicals) are responsible for NH3 and N2O emissions to air and NO3 emissions to surface and ground- water, as well as NH4 emissions to surface water. The long term emission factors for chemical fertilizers, digestate, and compost as de- scribed by Yoshida et al. (2016) were used to take into account the field emissions. Moreover, the same approach was also applied taking ac- count the avoided field emissions caused by the substitution of chemical fertilizers with organic fertilizers. The marginal chemical fertilizers, as described in detailed by Tonini et al. (2016), were considered when modeling the substitution of organic nitrogen for chemical fertilizers. The marginal chemical fertilizer which will be substituted by nitrogen from compost and digestate was urea. A sensitivity analysis was also performed to check how the total performance would be changed as a results of changes in above-mentioned emission factors.

38: Life cycle impact assessment is used to aggregate the inventory data into some impact categories and damage categories. The method "IMPACT2002 " was chosen in this context to evaluate the combined effect of digestate treatment technologies and their related up- and down-stream processes. "IMPACT2002+" enables comparing

developed scenarios based on four damage categories: human health, ecosystem quality, climate change and resource consumption (Humbert et al., 2005). Human health is a very important indicator in this case study because emissions at different steps (biogas combustion, digestate storage, composting, field applications, etc.) all endanger human beings. As a simple example, the emission of PM2.5 during the trans- portation of PM to biogas plants as well as the transportation of di- gestate to farms is a serious risk for human health, thus worthy of in- vestigation. Ecosystem quality is also important is this context, because nitrogen leaching from digestate would enter to the water resources and deteriorate water quality. Therefore, they also have impacts on eco- system quality.

Apart from four damage categories mentioned above, water foot- print was also taken into account in this study. The water footprint is a measure of humanity's appropriation of fresh water in volumes of water consumed and/or polluted. The water footprint would show the extent of water use in relation to consumption by each scenario.

40: Developed scenarios are compared in four damage categories and the results are shown in Table 9. Having compared all scenarios, baseline scenario still was a better option for the anaerobic digestion of PM in the selected area. It means that, the direct use of digestate on the farmlands, if not over-supplied and not impose additional transporta- tion cost on farmers, would be the best choice. As seen in this figure, coupled anaerobic digestion and microalgae production under the conditions of DL outperformed other digestate treatment technologies in three damage categories, i.e., human health, ecosystem quality, and climate change, while in resources damage category PBF had the best performance. The results are discussed in details in the following sub- sections and are followed by a series of sensitivity analysis to give better insights into the outcomes achieved.
41: As shown in Table 9, baseline scenario had the best environmental performance, i.e., 3.10E-05 DALY/FU, in human health damage cate- gory followed by DL, i.e., 3.77E-05 DALY/FU. In this damage category, PBF dominated other scenarios and had the highest environmental impacts among others compared. What made PBF the worst digestate treatment option could be listed as PBF production unit process and digestate storage phase (Table 9). More specifically, sodium hydroxide, consumed as pH adjustment material to increase the efficiency of ni- trogen crystallization in PBF process, was the most important con- tributor to this damage category. In another words, 88% of the total impact from PBF unit attributed to the NaOH and is originated from its production stage in the background system (Fig. S1). Digestate storage, to a lesser extent, also contributed to the human health damage cate- gory of PBF (Fig. 5). In other words, ammonia emissions during di- gestate storage exacerbated the human health damages of PBF as a digestate treatment technology.
Compared to PBF, the baseline scenario was affected most with
Overall damages caused by four scenario.
Percentage shows the comparison of scenarios compared to the best one in each damage category.
Fig. 6. Contribution of different processes to the environmental impacts of baseline scenario.

ammonia emissions from digestate storage (Fig. 6). In baseline scenario, almost 45% of the total impact on the human health damage category originated from NH3 emissions. The case study biogas plant currently employs an open tank to store the digestate which is produced out of cultivation seasons. This type of storage system has the highest am- monia and methane emissions with considerable impacts on different damage categories. The replacement of open tanks with other types of storage system will be further investigated and discussed under sensi- tivity analysis.

The transportation of digestate to farmlands was the second im- portant contributor to human health damage category associated with baseline scenario. The negative environmental impacts of digestate transportation had a positive correlation with the average distance between farmlands and biogas plants. To decrease such negative en- vironmental impacts, one option could be constructing decentralized biogas plants to decrease the distance between farms and plants, however other techno-economic aspects including the production of capital equipment should be also considered in this context. In a recent study conducted by Tsapekos et al. (2019), biomethane fueled trans- portation machines were shown to be a good alternative to diesel fueled trucks to decrease the negative environmental impact of transportation process associated with biogas production. They showed that, for a given transportation distance, biogas/biomethane fueled trucks had lower exhaust emissions.

Microalgae production under DL circumstances showed better en- vironmental performance than its cultivation under the conditions of IFBCO. The total impact caused by scenario 2 was calculated at 5.61E- 05 DALY/FU of which 41% and 14% attributed to digestate storage and flocculation process, respectively (Fig. 7). The former impact can be decreased either by using covered/sealed storage systems or ideally by a fast-continuous processing at down-stream which reduces de- pendency on storage systems and its subsequent emissions. What dif- ferentiated between IFBCO and DL was the use of flocculants with significant impacts at the background system. More specifically, PAC production for further use in the flocculation system affected human health damage category, causing that DL outperformed IFBCO for mi- croalgae production.

46: In ecosystem quality damage category, coupled AD and microalgae production outperformed other scenarios while DL and IFBCO ranked

first and second, respectively. Based on the results achieved herein, the environmental damages of PBF production scenario to the ecosystem quality was calculated at 4.65E + 01 PDF/m2.yr.l of which 60% attributed to the PBF unit. The PBF process was highly affected by the use of NaOH in crystallization process contributing to the total impact by PBF unit. In other words, the share of NaOH in the total impact of PBF unit was estimated at 88% of the total impact. Open tank storage system with significant ammonia emissions to air was known as the second most important contributor to ecosystem quality of PBF with a share of 17%. Although the avoided environmental impacts, caused by the substitution of chemical fertilizer with organic fertilizer and those of natural gas with biogas, led to a saving of - 10.80 PDF/m² yr.FU, it was not enough to compensate the adverse environmental impacts of other processes.

In ecosystem quality damage category of baseline scenario, trans- portation played a pivotal role and constituted almost 37% of the total impact. The detailed analysis (Table S4) showed that the exhaust emissions, among others, copper, zinc, nitrogen oxides, and chromium were the most important contributors to ecosystem quality damage category. The results showed that, ammonia emissions from digestate storage tank ranked second among the contributors to ecosystem quality damage category of baseline scenario. In this scenario, the substitution of chemical fertilizers with digestate would led to savings in this damage category, however, the savings achieved were not en- ough to neutral the environmental damages. It is also worth mentioning that baseline scenario was evaluated under proper digestate application rate on the farmlands. The oversupply of digestate in the considered area would bring about excessive gas and non-point source pollutions, thus even exacerbate the environmental performance of baseline sce- nario.

In this damage category, DL and IFBCO had the best environmental performance, respectively, however there were also more possibilities for decreasing their environmental emissions. In both scenarios (Figs. 7 and 8) digestate storage needed considerable attention since open tank was not a proper choice for the storage of biologically active digestate. The ammonia which is released from stored digestate, if saved by fast and continuous down-stream process, can be assimilated by microalgae and participated in biomass production. Consequently, more biomass will be produced leading to higher biogas production during co-diges- tion with PM.

48: In ecosystem quality damage category, coupled AD and microalgae production outperformed other scenarios while DL and IFBCO ranked

first and second, respectively. Based on the results achieved herein, the environmental damages of PBF production scenario to the ecosystem quality was calculated at 4.65E + 01 PDF/m2.yr.l of which 60% attributed to the PBF unit. The PBF process was highly affected by the use of NaOH in crystallization process contributing to the total impact by PBF unit. In other words, the share of NaOH in the total impact of PBF unit was estimated at 88% of the total impact. Open tank storage system with significant ammonia emissions to air was known as the second most important contributor to ecosystem quality of PBF with a share of 17%. Although the avoided environmental impacts, caused by the substitution of chemical fertilizer with organic fertilizer and those of natural gas with biogas, led to a saving of - 10.80 PDF/m² yr.FU, it was not enough to compensate the adverse environmental impacts of other processes.

In ecosystem quality damage category of baseline scenario, trans- portation played a pivotal role and constituted almost 37% of the total impact. The detailed analysis (Table S4) showed that the exhaust emissions, among others, copper, zinc, nitrogen oxides, and chromium were the most important contributors to ecosystem quality damage category. The results showed that, ammonia emissions from digestate storage tank ranked second among the contributors to ecosystem quality damage category of baseline scenario. In this scenario, the substitution of chemical fertilizers with digestate would led to savings in this damage category, however, the savings achieved were not en- ough to neutral the environmental damages. It is also worth mentioning that baseline scenario was evaluated under proper digestate application rate on the farmlands. The oversupply of digestate in the considered area would bring about excessive gas and non-point source pollutions, thus even exacerbate the environmental performance of baseline sce- nario.

In this damage category, DL and IFBCO had the best environmental performance, respectively, however there were also more possibilities for decreasing their environmental emissions. In both scenarios (Figs. 7 and 8) digestate storage needed considerable attention since open tank was not a proper choice for the storage of biologically active digestate. The ammonia which is released from stored digestate, if saved by fast and continuous down-stream process, can be assimilated by microalgae and participated in biomass production. Consequently, more biomass will be produced leading to higher biogas production during co-diges- tion with PM.

49: In this damage category, baseline scenario outperformed other scenarios and led to a net saving of - 1.10E + 01 kg CO2-eq/FU (Table 9). The avoided environmental impacts made by the substitution of natural gas with biogas and those of chemical fertilizer with organic fertilizers constituted almost 55% of the total impact. Although the same substitutions were also applied for other scenarios, the avoided environmental impacts made in IFBCO to PBF were not big enough to neutral the environmental damages caused by other processes, thus they did not lead to emission savings. The results obtained in this study was also in agreement with the finding of Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2015). They compared different digestate treatment strategies including drying and pelletizing, composting, and ammonia stripping. Having compared with conventional digestate treatment, evaluated scenarios sub- stantially increased global warming impacts with damages on climate change.

In this environmental index, DL ranked second with a net impact of 3.55E + 01 kg CO2-eq/FU. In this scenario, methane emissions from storage system as well as the emission of CH4 and N2O from composting process were introduced as the most important polluters as they com- prised almost 50% of the total impact. Greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from composting process, me- thane emissions form storage tank, as well as GHG emissions from background system during PAC production made IFBCO as the worst digestate treatment option in this damage category. Although covered/ sealed storage systems, and ideally a fast-continuous down-stream process, can possibly reduce GHG emissions and consequently improve the environmental outcomes, PAC production and its associated emis- sions in the background system remains a serious challenge for this scenario and require future research to find a more environment- friendly substitute for that.
Fig. 8. Contribution of different processes to the environmental impacts of DL.

51: Resources damage category refers to the primary energy consump- tion and is expressed based on MJ primary energy. This damage cate- gory was considered as part of the energy flow analysis of the system and is of high importance since only renewable energy system with negative energy flows (energy saving) would ensure the energy payback (Rahimi et al., 2018). The results obtained showed that all scenarios were energetically favorable because they led to the saving of primary energy. The energy saving in the baseline scenario was the highest while that of IFBCO was the lowest. This was in line with the findings of Rehl and Müller (2011) who compared seven options for biogas di- gestate treatment including conventional management, composting, belt dryer, drum dryer, solar dryer, and physical chemical treatment. They showed that conventional digestate treatment had the lowest primary energy demand compared with other treatment options SO may help to have the highest energy saving. Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2015) also reported that conventional digestate treatment had the lowest energy and mineral use compared with other treatment alternatives.

The energy consumption associated with the background system of IFBCO, i.e., PAC production, was estimated at 345 MJ/FU, while the total energy demand was found to be 538 MJ/FU (Fig. S2). The high energy payback from co-digestion of PM and microalgae under the conditions of IFBCO resulted in an energy saving of 1.68E + 03 MJ/ FU. Put it all together the net energy saving achieved under IFBCO circumstances was calculated at -1.45E + 03 MJ/FU, ranking this scenario fourth among others.

The energy recovered from the co-digestion of PM and microalgae under DL circumstances, i.e., the biogas produced to substitute natural gas, was estimated at 1.68E + 03 MJ/FU, even higher than what achieve from PBF, i.e., 1.30E + 03 MJ/FU (Fig. S3). What dis- tinguished DL and PBF was the highest energy payback from the sub- stitution of PBF with chemical fertilizer (-7.34E + 02 MJ/FU) and that of electricity production in the CHP plant with fossil-based elec- tricity (-3.37E + 02 MJ/FU). Such differences caused DL stood at the third rank while PBF ranked second. It is worth mentioning that higher energy payback from PBF was compensated with lower environmental performance under other damage categories, therefore such higher energy recovery could not be considered as a standalone benchmark for policy makers.
