54: Water resources are limited SO it has been getting more important to know how much water would be consumed by any given system when system sustainability is under consideration (Tian et al., 2018). Water footprint is an ecological index which helps to determine how much direct and indirect water is used by a given activity (Gerbens-Leenes and Hoekstra, 2012). Accordingly, water footprint of the developed scenarios was also calculated and compared to have better insight into the sustainability of processes.

The results obtained in the present study demonstrated that coupled AD and microalgae cultivation at down-stream were the best scenarios in terms of water footprint, while IFBCO and DL ranked first and second, respectively (Table S3). The index of water footprint for IFBCO and DL was calculated at -0.52, and 0.46 3/FU, respectively. The negative values show that these scenarios could help save water re- sources since the process water remained at the end of the cycle had high quality to be used in agro-industrial sector. Biogas plants normally use fresh water to decrease the TS content of feedstocks such manure and organic waste. However, the digestate remaining after AD process is used as organic fertilizer. Under such circumstances, digestate is considered as a soil conditioner which substitutes for chemical fertili- zers and not irrigation water. On the contrary, IFBCO and its con- sequent microalgae cultivation removes nutrient and organic matters from liquid fraction of digestate. Therefore, the remaining water has

low organic matter while has higher quality. Accordingly, the re- maining water could be considered as process water with various ap- plications such as irrigation water. If used as irrigation water, it could be considered to substitute part of fresh water which is currently used in agricultural sectors. The performance of IFBCO technology is similar to what happens in waste water treatment plant. In other words, IFBCO technology decrease the organic matter content and remove COD from the liquid fraction of digestate, followed by a nutrient removal step, i.e., microalgae cultivation. Therefore, the process water would have an acceptable quality to be used in agricultural and also some other in- dustrial activities, replacing fresh water in such activities. Compared to IFBCO, DL required more water since simultaneous separation and di- lution method was used to decrease the turbidity of liquid digestate. The higher fresh water which was required to dilute the media for microalgae cultivation put DL at the second ranked among other sce- narios taken into account.

Water footprint associated with baseline scenario was estimated at 0.28 /FU, putting the baseline scenario at the third rank. Although digestate was considered to be applied on farmlands, it was used to substitute chemical fertilizers rather than to replace fresh water in agricultural production. Therefore, the baseline scenario had positive water footprint. In PBF, the remaining water after PBF production contained sulfuric acid and high concentration of NaOH, making it unsuitable to be used as process water. Therefore, PBF had the highest water footprint among scenarios taken into account.

The results proved that coupled AD and microalgae production not only could improve energy recovery from PM management by higher renewable energy production, but also save water resources and de- crease stress on water resource depletion. However, further research is still required to investigate to which extent the use of process water in agricultural production would affect product's quality and the asso- ciated yield.

58: The results discussed in the previous sections clearly showed that open storage systems significantly influenced damage categories by different contributions. They could directly affect human health, eco- system, and climate change damage categories due to high ammonia and methane emissions. Moreover, such emissions indirectly influenced resources damage category due to carbon and nitrogen loses and con- sequently due to decreased energy recovery. Covered storage tanks and ideally sealed systems can significantly decrease emissions from di- gestate and improve energy and nutrient recovery (Styles et al., 2018). To see how the results achieved under initial assumptions were sensi- tive to the choice of storage system, a sensitivity analysis was per- formed and open tanks were replaced with sealed systems.

As illustrated in Fig. 9, the improvement of environmental perfor- mance in human health damage categories varied from 25% to 165%, with the highest in baseline scenario and the lowest in PBF. Such im- provement led to a net saving of 2.018E-05, and 1.59E-05 DALY/ FU in baseline scenario and DL, respectively, and a decreased impact of 3.09E-06, and 1.52E-04 DALY/FU in IFBCO and PBF, respectively. In this damage category, an improvement of up to 57% was noticed in DL while the total impact reached to 7.42E + 00 PDF/m².yr. As dis- cussed earlier, PBF had the worst performance in this damage category. The use of sealed tank for this scenario decreased the total impacts by 20%, reaching to 3.73E + 01 PDF/m². In climate change damage category, a high reduction of up to 273% was observed in baseline scenario, leading to a net saving of -4.11E + 01 kg CO2-eq/FU. the second improvement in this damage category by 89% reaching to 3.73E + 00 kg CO2-eq/FU. In the damage category of ecosystem quality, the improvement in environmental savings varied from 20% to 57%, with the highest for DL scenario and the lowest for PBF. This has been achieved due to better nutrient recycling in the system and its
Fig. 9. Improvements in damage categories under sealed digestate storage system. Columns show the percentage of improvement and black dots represent the damages.

transformation from liquid digestate to microalgae. Such better nutrient recycling also partially improved energy recovery due to higher bio- mass production which led to 1% to 3% improvements in Resource damage category.

There are some conflicting reports regarding the amount of CH4 production from digestate when stored under open tank and covered tank systems. Rodhe et al., measured the daily CH4 production during summer storage of digested cattle slurry as 7.79 and 6.78 g CH4-C/m³ in open tank and covered tank systems, respectively (Rodhe et al., 2015). They also reported that there was no significant difference between open tanks and covered tanks in terms of methane production during winter storage, while the daily production was also very low. This is also in line with the IPCC guidelines for national greenhouse gas in- ventories mentioning that higher CH4 production should be considered for open storage systems (IPCC, 2006). However, Willien et al., showed that covered storage system would increase CH4 production from di- gested sewage sludge during storage in covered system (Willén et al., 2016). This has been attributed to the anaerobic conditions in the covered tanks. Regardless of if CH4 production in a covered storage system is increased or decreased, a covered tank with a gas collection system would decrease CH4 emissions into the air. In other words, the CH4 production is not important in this case but the emissions into the air are problematic since contributes to climate change damage cate- gory. Regarding N2O production, covered system due to lower aeration decreases N2O formation from digestate, however, there is an agree- ment on low N2O emissions during storage period are negligible (Rodhe et al., 2015; Willén et al., 2016).
3.6.2. Sensitivity to emission factors Microalgae production, and more specifically algae production

under DL circumstances showed promising results as a digestate treat- ment technology at downstream when the storage and direct use of digestate must be limited. The use of compost, produced from the solid fraction of digestate, as well as the liquid fertilizer, from microalgae unit, in agricultural production would lead to NH3, N2O, NO3 , and NH4 emissions. These emissions may increase under certain conditions or decrease under controlled application. Therefore, in this section a sensitivity analysis was performed to investigate how 25 and 50% in- crease and decrease in above-mentioned emissions would affect the total performance of DL. The same also applied for DL under sealed tank storage system.

As can be seen in Fig. 10, climate change damage category showed the highest sensitivity to the drift of field emissions from farm appli- cation of digestate-based soil amendment. Based on the results achieved herein, a 25% reduction in the emission factors would lead to the 9.33% reduction in the total impact while a 25% increase, raised the total impact by 11.11%. Under the worst condition, if emission factors in- creased by 50%, the total impact would raise by 21.32%. On the con- trary, ecosystem quality damage category showed a low sensitivity to the drift of field emissions where a 25% and 50% increase of field emission factors led to 1.05 and 3.49% higher total impact. While previous studies with focus on the oversupply of organic manure to the farmlands unanimously reported that the N leaching would increase in some cropping systems, they ignored to evaluate the drift in greenhouse gas emissions under such circumstances (Fan et al., 2017; Quan et al., 2015). The results achieve herein, proved that the role of greenhouse gas emissions from the filed application of organic soil amendment must not be underestimated. Based on the result achieved, increase or decrease on above-mentioned emissions would have no impact on Re- source damage category and also the impacts on human health was very

Fig. 10. Sensitivity analysis of DL (Sc-3) to different NH3, N2O, NO3 and NH4 emissions from compost and liquid fertilizer after farm application. 25%1 and 50%1 stand for 25% and 50% higher emissions, respectively; 25%J and 50% |represent 25% and 50% lower emissions; ST + 25%1, ST + 50%1, ST + 25%J, and ST + 50%|stand for changes in field emissions under sealed tank storage system.
small compared with their impacts on climate change.

As dis- cussed earlier, PBF had the worst performance in this damage category. The use of sealed tank for this scenario decreased the total impacts by 20%, reaching to 3.73E + 01 PDF/m². In climate change damage category, a high reduction of up to 273% was observed in baseline scenario, leading to a net saving of -4.11E + 01 kg CO2-eq/FU. the second improvement in this damage category by 89% reaching to 3.73E + 00 kg CO2-eq/FU. In the damage category of ecosystem quality, the improvement in environmental savings varied from 20% to 57%, with the highest for DL scenario and the lowest for PBF. This has been achieved due to better nutrient recycling in the system and its
Fig. 9. Improvements in damage categories under sealed digestate storage system. Columns show the percentage of improvement and black dots represent the damages.

transformation from liquid digestate to microalgae. Such better nutrient recycling also partially improved energy recovery due to higher bio- mass production which led to 1% to 3% improvements in Resource damage category.

There are some conflicting reports regarding the amount of CH4 production from digestate when stored under open tank and covered tank systems. Rodhe et al., measured the daily CH4 production during summer storage of digested cattle slurry as 7.79 and 6.78 g CH4-C/m³ in open tank and covered tank systems, respectively (Rodhe et al., 2015). They also reported that there was no significant difference between open tanks and covered tanks in terms of methane production during winter storage, while the daily production was also very low. This is also in line with the IPCC guidelines for national greenhouse gas in- ventories mentioning that higher CH4 production should be considered for open storage systems (IPCC, 2006). However, Willien et al., showed that covered storage system would increase CH4 production from di- gested sewage sludge during storage in covered system (Willén et al., 2016). This has been attributed to the anaerobic conditions in the covered tanks. Regardless of if CH4 production in a covered storage system is increased or decreased, a covered tank with a gas collection system would decrease CH4 emissions into the air. In other words, the CH4 production is not important in this case but the emissions into the air are problematic since contributes to climate change damage cate- gory. Regarding N2O production, covered system due to lower aeration decreases N2O formation from digestate, however, there is an agree- ment on low N2O emissions during storage period are negligible (Rodhe et al., 2015; Willén et al., 2016).
3.6.2. Sensitivity to emission factors Microalgae production, and more specifically algae production

under DL circumstances showed promising results as a digestate treat- ment technology at downstream when the storage and direct use of digestate must be limited. The use of compost, produced from the solid fraction of digestate, as well as the liquid fertilizer, from microalgae unit, in agricultural production would lead to NH3, N2O, NO3 , and NH4 emissions. These emissions may increase under certain conditions or decrease under controlled application. Therefore, in this section a sensitivity analysis was performed to investigate how 25 and 50% in- crease and decrease in above-mentioned emissions would affect the total performance of DL. The same also applied for DL under sealed tank storage system.

As can be seen in Fig. 10, climate change damage category showed the highest sensitivity to the drift of field emissions from farm appli- cation of digestate-based soil amendment. Based on the results achieved herein, a 25% reduction in the emission factors would lead to the 9.33% reduction in the total impact while a 25% increase, raised the total impact by 11.11%. Under the worst condition, if emission factors in- creased by 50%, the total impact would raise by 21.32%. On the con- trary, ecosystem quality damage category showed a low sensitivity to the drift of field emissions where a 25% and 50% increase of field emission factors led to 1.05 and 3.49% higher total impact. While previous studies with focus on the oversupply of organic manure to the farmlands unanimously reported that the N leaching would increase in some cropping systems, they ignored to evaluate the drift in greenhouse gas emissions under such circumstances (Fan et al., 2017; Quan et al., 2015). The results achieve herein, proved that the role of greenhouse gas emissions from the filed application of organic soil amendment must not be underestimated. Based on the result achieved, increase or decrease on above-mentioned emissions would have no impact on Re- source damage category and also the impacts on human health was very

small compared with their impacts on climate change.
60: The results discussed in the previous sections clearly showed that open storage systems significantly influenced damage categories by different contributions. They could directly affect human health, eco- system, and climate change damage categories due to high ammonia and methane emissions. Moreover, such emissions indirectly influenced resources damage category due to carbon and nitrogen loses and con- sequently due to decreased energy recovery. Covered storage tanks and ideally sealed systems can significantly decrease emissions from di- gestate and improve energy and nutrient recovery (Styles et al., 2018). To see how the results achieved under initial assumptions were sensi- tive to the choice of storage system, a sensitivity analysis was per- formed and open tanks were replaced with sealed systems.

As can be seen in Fig. 10, climate change damage category showed the highest sensitivity to the drift of field emissions from farm appli- cation of digestate-based soil amendment. Based on the results achieved herein, a 25% reduction in the emission factors would lead to the 9.33% reduction in the total impact while a 25% increase, raised the total impact by 11.11%. Under the worst condition, if emission factors in- creased by 50%, the total impact would raise by 21.32%. On the con- trary, ecosystem quality damage category showed a low sensitivity to the drift of field emissions where a 25% and 50% increase of field emission factors led to 1.05 and 3.49% higher total impact. While previous studies with focus on the oversupply of organic manure to the farmlands unanimously reported that the N leaching would increase in some cropping systems, they ignored to evaluate the drift in greenhouse gas emissions under such circumstances (Fan et al., 2017; Quan et al., 2015). The results achieve herein, proved that the role of greenhouse gas emissions from the filed application of organic soil amendment must not be underestimated. Based on the result achieved, increase or decrease on above-mentioned emissions would have no impact on Re- source damage category and also the impacts on human health was very

Fig. 10. Sensitivity analysis of DL (Sc-3) to different NH3, N2O, NO3 and NH4 emissions from compost and liquid fertilizer after farm application. 25%1 and 50%1 stand for 25% and 50% higher emissions, respectively; 25%J and 50% |represent 25% and 50% lower emissions; ST + 25%1, ST + 50%1, ST + 25%J, and ST + 50%|stand for changes in field emissions under sealed tank storage system.
small compared with their impacts on climate change.
65: The transportation of PM to biogas plant as well as digestate, and process water from biogas plant to farmlands considerably contributed to different damage categories, and their contributions were slightly higher for baseline scenario and DL. Therefore, in this section we in- vestigated how much improvement or deterioration would be attained under different transportation scenarios. The initial distance used for the present study was 20 km representing the average distance between Xibaidian biogas plant and its surrounding farms. The maximum dis- tance allowance, which is normally used for transportation of biomass to bio-processing plants such as biogas plants or biorefineries, is 100 km which in most cases, as reported by Rajaeifar et al. (2016) and Khoshnevisan et al. (2018a), imposes unreasonable environmental costs. Accordingly, 100 km was tested as the highest limit, followed by 50 and 30 km. The lowest distance, which is reported as optimal dis- tance between farms and biogas plants, is 5 km (Styles et al., 2018), thereby the effect of 5 and 10 km transportation on the overall results was also investigated (Van Stappen et al., 2016).

In baseline scenario, climate change damage category showed the highest sensitivity to transportation distance where a distance of 5 km decreased GHG emissions by 118% while 100 km increased them by 629% (Fig. 11). Compared to climate change, human health damage category showed lower sensitivity, for which distances of 5 and 10 km led to 54% and 36% reduction of damages, respectively. On the con- trary, ecosystem quality and climate change were the most sensitive damage categories for DL (Fig. S5). Although, the decreased mean distance between farms and biogas plants would help improve the

environmental performance of the evaluated scenarios, that was not enough to compensate damages from other processes and led to savings in these damage categories. However, the results achieved emphasized that the location of biogas plants is of importance and must be carefully selected when a new biogas plant is planned to be constructed. The results achieved was also in line with those reported by Tsapekos et al. (2019). They showed that how a total distance of 75 km and higher for all necessary transportations, along with other factors such as low methane yield, would result in failure in the environmental outcomes of a bioenergy system. In this context, Dresen and Jandewerth (2012) integrated geoinformation system, biomass potentials, cost information, technology database, and LCA to build up an spatial life cycle in- ventory, to introduce the most suitable locations for constructing new biogas plants. As shown herein, an average distance more than 50 km can deteriorate the environmental performance up to 100%, making AD an unsuitable option for agricultural waste management.

68: AD is a well-known and promoted biological waste management technology with two final products, namely biogas and digestate. Biogas is regarded as a renewable source of energy with various stab- lished downstream utilization pathways including upgrading to trans- portation fuel grad bio-methane, and combined heat and power pro- duction (Khoshnevisan et al., 2016; Mohammadi et al., 2019). On the contrary, the direct use of digestate on farmlands, following a 3 to 6- month storage period, is still the most common practice to handle the huge amount of biogas slurry produced. Although beneficial in terms of increasing soil organic matter, providing micro and macro nutrient,

Fig. 11. The influence of different transportation distance between biogas plant and farms on different damage categories in baseline scenario. Columns represent the percentage of improvement/deterioration and black dots shows damages to damage category.

etc., many scientists have warned about its direct use on farmlands since it would increase nutrient leaching and damage ecosystem and human (Nkoa, 2014). Moreover, the higher water content of AD di- gestate makes its long-transportation costly and uneconomic. There- fore, no doubt that there is an urgent need to find alternative pathways for its upcycling, especially when digestate certification schemes and digestate use legislations are taken more seriously.

The results of the present study proved that no individual down- stream treatment could dominate others in all damage categories. Hence, many parameters must be taken into account when trying to introduce the best utilization pathway for a given region. Among sce- narios compared herein, the scenario including digestate fractionation into solid and liquid and their use for composting and microalgae cul- tivation, respectively, would be a suitable downstream strategy with lower impacts on human health, ecosystem quality, and climate change damage category. However, the high turbidity of digestate after solid- liquid separation may hinder microalgae production and increase fresh water requirements. Therefore, its water footprint would be proble- matic and the energy payback would decrease due to lower microalgae production. The high turbidity of liquid digestate caused by suspended materials is a major issue, although microalgal cultivation can partly reduce the turbidity by removing the suspended materials (Xia and Murphy, 2016). High turbidity would be a serious challenge for scaled- up microalgae production, so distinctive digestate such as filtration, centrifugation, an precipitation, have been suggested. The results of the present study showed that, IFBCO with some modifications (such as

sealed tank storage system or fast-continuous operation) could poten- tially be a suitable choice. This digestate treatment technology had the lowest water footprint due to its sever operations with a high COD removal rate, delivering high quality process water for agricultural production. Although energetically favorable, IFBCO had the lowest net energy production. The results signified that PAC, used in IFBCO sce- nario, carried a great amount of environmental burdens from back- ground system, thus further research must be undertaken to identify more environmental friendly flocculants. From a technical point of view, it should be noted that microalgae cultivation faces serious challenges which could affect the overall performance of system. Am- monia inhibition is one of the concerns which should be taken into account (Xia and Murphy, 2016). High total ammonia nitrogen at high concentrations may lead to inhibition depending on the microalgae species and cultivation conditions. Moreover, if optimal N/P mass ratio, i.e., 7, is not provided during the cultivation period, the growth rate can reach acceptable level while sufficient addition of phosphate could double growth rate. Bacterial contamination is another concern may greatly inhibit the growth of target microalgae. It is worth mentioning that life cycle costs, social LCA, exergetic performance, local or regional schemes or legislations, etc. must be taken into account when a suitable technology is chosen for a given area. Multi-objective optimization methods and superstructure-based optimization approach have been introduced as systematic procedure to help policy makers in this context (Rizwan et al., 2018; Vadenbo et al., 2014). Such models can make a balance between different aspects of
sustainability, i.e., energy, environment, and economy, to identify the optimal processing pathways for any given biomass.

73: Simultaneous digestate treatment and microalgae production under the conditions of DL scenario showed promising results from a life cycle assessment point of views with more possibilities for future improve- ments. The co-digestion of PM and microalgae led to a saving of 1.61E + 03 MJ/primary energy per tonne of PM, making this scenario energetically favorable. Moreover, compared with direct use of diges- tate on farmlands, DL could decrease damages to ecosystem quality by 40%. Compared with IFBCO and PBF scenarios, DL decreased negative impacts on human health damage category, respectively, by 32% and 116%. The results of the present study showed that, IFBCO would be a suitable alternative when water footprint is the main concern. IFBCO could also dominant the baseline scenario in ecosystem quality damage category, leading to 16.7% lower impacts. Considering IFBCO tech- nology, attempts must be made to identify flocculants with lower im- pacts on the background systems. PAC used for flocculation process accounted for 14 to 27% of the total environmental impacts in different damage categories which needs to be properly decreased. When mi- croalgae show sensitivity to cultivation media turbidity, IFBCO tech- nology is capable of removing COD, SS, and ammonia at high rate, ensuring a high energy payback from PM-microalgae co-digestion. Although promising results were achieved in terms of microalgae pro- duction scenarios, the scenario including direct use of digestate on farmlands was still more environmentally favorable option provided that digestate is not oversupplied to the farms. Moreover, it is also highly suggested to consider emerging contaminants including both chemical and microbial into the analyses of resource recovery and end use of recovered products in agricultural activities to make sure such contaminants would not endanger human beings and ecosystem. Last but not least, IFBCO coupled with microalgae production is at R&D stage, thus the results would be tentative due to high uncertainty. Future research is still required to deal with such uncertainty and in- troduce the most sustainable digestate treatment technologies.
